The official MCP documentation provides a debugging guide and even a workshop video for troubleshooting complex integrations. In maintenance, ensure you keep your MCP servers updated to the latest protocol version - as MCP evolves, there may be updates needed for compatibility. Periodically review and update credentials (e.g., if an API key is nearing expiration, update it in the server config proactively). Finally,", "metadata": {"title": "MCP Servers: A Comprehensive Guide\u200a\u2014\u200aAnother way to explain", "summary": "**TL;DR:**\n\n### Main Facts:\n1. **Technology Overview**: MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers enable AI systems to connect with external tools and data sources through a standardized interface, enhancing their capabilities.\n2. **Usefulness**: MCP servers allow AI models to access live data and perform actions in real-time, bridging the gap between AI and real-world applications.\n3. **Implementation**: Setting up an MCP server involves installing server software, configuring it, and connecting it to an AI client, with many pre-built options available.\n4. **Code Examples**: Developers can use SDKs to create MCP servers with minimal code, such as using Cloudflare's Workers integration.\n5. **Best Practices**: Emphasizes modularity, security, and performance optimization in the design and deployment of MCP servers.\n\n### Abstract:\nThe article provides a comprehensive overview of MCP servers, which serve as connectors for AI systems to interact with external resources. Introduced by Anthropic in 2024, MCP servers have gained traction across various industries, allowing AI applications to become more context-aware. The article details the technical architecture of MCP, including its client-host-server model, and offers guidance on setting up and configuring MCP servers. It also includes code examples and best practices for effective implementation.\n\n### Implementation Outline:\n1. **Setup**: Install or deploy the MCP server software, either by using pre-built options or building a custom server.\n2. **Configuration**: Configure the server with necessary settings and credentials to connect to the AI client.\n3. **Development**: Use SDKs to define server endpoints and methods, facilitating easy integration with AI applications.\n4. **Testing**: Ensure the server functions correctly by testing the connection and data retrieval processes.\n5. **Deployment**: Deploy the server locally or on cloud services, ensuring it adheres to the MCP protocol for seamless communication.\n\n### Code Excerpts:\n```python\n# Example code snippet demonstrating a key feature\ndef setup_mcp_server():\n    # Pseudo code to illustrate the concept\n    install_server_software()  # Install the MCP server\n    configure_settings()        # Configure server settings\n    define_endpoints()         # Define API endpoints for the server\n    start_server()             # Start the MCP server\n    return \"MCP Server is running\"\n```\n\n### Best Practices:\n- **Optimization**: Use lightweight services and avoid tight coupling to enhance performance.\n- **Maintainability**: Write clean, modular code to facilitate easy updates and changes.\n- **Security**: Ensure that only authorized clients can connect to the MCP servers to protect sensitive data.\n\n### Additional Resources:\n- [GitHub Library for MCP Servers](https://github.com/Anthropic/mcp)\n- [Cloudflare Workers MCP Integration](https://github.com/cloudflare/workers-mcp)\n\nFor more detailed information, refer to the [Main Article URL](https://medium.com/data-and-beyond/mcp-servers-a-comprehensive-guide-another-way-to-explain-67c2fa58f650).", "web_address": "https://medium.com/data-and-beyond/mcp-servers-a-comprehensive-guide-another-way-to-explain-67c2fa58f650"}}, {"id": "60ad666e-3276-11f0-8d70-fcb3bcabaf11", "text": "> And if you appreciate this content and want to support my work, please give me 50 claps and consider buying me a coffee via [Buy Me A Coffee](https://buymeacoffee.com/toniramchandani?l=en). Thank you for your support, and let's dive in!\n\n---\n\n## Introducing the MCP Inspector\n\nBehind every successful AI agent is a series of invisible calls, server responses, and protocols negotiating in silence. But when something doesn't work - or even when it _does_ - you need visibility. Enter the **MCP Inspector**: a lightweight visual debugger and test bench that lets developers peek under the hood of their MCP servers.\n\n> Think of it as the browser DevTools for your AI agent's brain.", "metadata": {"summary": "summarize this article:ARTICLEURL=https://medium.com/data-and-beyond/build-your-first-mcp-server-with-python-sdk-mcp-inspector-ae2c5ca3b36c\n# Build Your First MCP Server with Python SDK & MCP Inspector\n\n> Imagine asking your AI assistant: 'Should I carry an umbrella today in Pune?' - and getting back an accurate, real-time answer powered by your very own tool. That's the magic of MCP servers. And today, you'll build one from scratch.\n\n![](https://miro.medium.com/0*H3htTJGgZ56FBnOi)\n\n## Why Build an MCP Server?\n\nIt all starts with a simple need - context. Large Language Models are brilliant at reasoning, summarizing, and generating content. But left in isolation, they lack something crucial: **real-world awareness**.\n\nThat's where **MCP servers** step in.\n\nImagine an LLM as your star employee. Now give that employee access to tools - browsers, APIs, databases, 3D engines. That's what MCP does. It's a bridge between language and execution. And MCP servers? They're the endpoints that expose those tools in a standardized way.\n\nWe have covered all many things about MCP see those pieces below\n\n> [**The Model Context Protocol (MCP): The Ultimate Guide**](https://medium.com/data-and-beyond/the-model-context-protocol-mcp-the-ultimate-guide-c40539e2a8e7)\n\n> [**MCP Servers: A Comprehensive Guide - Another way to explain**](https://medium.com/data-and-beyond/mcp-servers-a-comprehensive-guide-another-way-to-explain-67c2fa58f650)\n\nIn this piece, we are going to build your own MCP server - a weather agent that talks to the [Open-Meteo API](https://open-meteo.com/), fetches forecasts, and returns structured data back to the model. No magic. No black boxes. Just Python, clear protocols, and raw weather intelligence.\n\nWe'll be using the official **Python SDK** for MCP (via the `mcp` package) and wrapping it all in a minimal but powerful CLI-ready microserver.\n\n---\n\n> Before we proceed further, I'd like to invite you on a deeper exploration into the world of generative AI with my book, **\"A Generative Journey to AI\"**.\n\n> This book is your ultimate guide to mastering the foundations and cutting-edge innovations in generative AI, offering insights and practical strategies to transform your understanding and work in the field.\n\n> You can grab your copy on [Amazon](https://www.amazon.in/Generative-Journey-Mastering-foundations-generative/dp/9365890845).\n\n> And if you appreciate this content and want to support my work, please give me 50 claps and consider buying me a coffee via [Buy Me A Coffee](https://buymeacoffee.com/toniramchandani?l=en). Thank you for your support, and let's dive in!\n\n---\n\n## Introducing the MCP Inspector\n\nBehind every successful AI agent is a series of invisible calls, server responses, and protocols negotiating in silence. But when something doesn't work - or even when it _does_ - you need visibility. Enter the **MCP Inspector**: a lightweight visual debugger and test bench that lets developers peek under the hood of their MCP servers.\n\n> Think of it as the browser DevTools for your AI agent's brain.\n\nIn this part of the article, we'll explore how the MCP Inspector fits into your development workflow, how to launch and use it, and what makes it indispensable when building or testing custom MCP tools. Whether you're inspecting tool responses, analyzing real-time logs, or simply trying to understand how your agent is interacting with your server, this visual interface puts you in control.\n\n## But first, what exactly is it?\n\nThe MCP Inspector is a locally hosted interface designed to test, debug, and simulate interactions between your language model agent and the MCP server it's connected to. Instead of blindly writing logs or printing JSON responses, you can watch your tools in action - click by click, response by response.\n\nNo more guesswork. Just clean, structured observability.\n\n---\n\n## \ud83d\udd27  Prerequisites\n\nYou'll need:\n\n- **Python 3.11+**\n\n- A code editor (VS Code recommended)\n\n- MCP Python SDK (`mcp[cli]`)\n\n- uv - a blazing-fast Python package manager\n\n- Optional: [Claude Desktop](https://claude.ai) to test your MCP locally\n\nIf you are interesred in Claude Desktop we have a guide on how to set it up on local machine\n\n> [**Setting Up MCP Servers in Claude Desktop**](https://medium.com/data-and-beyond/setting-up-mcp-servers-in-claude-desktop-a93a1baa862d)\n\n### Quick Setup with `uv`\n\nLet's keep things clean and modular:\n\n```bash\n# Create your project\nmkdir mcp-server-weather && cd mcp-server-weather\n```\n\n```bash\n# Initialize the project using uv\nuv init\nuv venv\nsource .venv/bin/activate\n# Install the Python MCP SDK and dependencies\nuv add \"mcp[cli]\" httpx\n```\n\n> It's time to put theory into practice. After exploring what MCP servers can do, we're now stepping into the workshop. This isn't just another tutorial - it's a deep-dive into how to build your own **MCP-enabled weather agent** using the **Open-Meteo API** and the **Python SDK for MCP**. Think of this as your first hands-on agent that blends live weather data with LLM reasoning.\n\n## Let's Break Down the MCP Weather Agent Code\n\nBelow, we walk through the weather MCP server piece by piece, explaining not just what each part does, but _why_ it's structured this way - especially in the context of how language models use MCP tools.\n\nCreate a server.py file and write the below code into that - as simple as that\n\n### 1. Imports and Initialization\n\n```python\nfrom typing import Any\nimport httpx\nfrom mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP\n```\n\n```python\nmcp = FastMCP(\"weather\")\n```\n\n- `typing.Any`: Gives us flexibility for dynamic API responses.\n\n- `httpx`: An async-friendly HTTP client to call the Open-Meteo API.\n\n- `FastMCP`: This is the Python SDK's core class to define and run an MCP server.\n\nWe initialize `FastMCP` with a name (`\"weather\"`). This name helps identify this server when multiple are used, and tags the tools under a namespace.\n\n### 2. Constants and HTTP Wrapper\n\n```python\nOPENMETEO_API_BASE = \"https://api.open-meteo.com/v1\"\nUSER_AGENT = \"weather-app/1.0\"\n```\n\n```python\nasync def make_openmeteo_request(url: str) -> dict[str, Any] | None:\n    headers = {\n        \"User-Agent\": USER_AGENT,\n        \"Accept\": \"application/json\"\n    }\n    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:\n        try:\n            response = await client.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=30.0)\n            response.raise_for_status()\n            return response.json()\n        except Exception:\n            return None\n```\n\n- This function abstracts away API requests. This helps with **code reuse**, but also **error isolation**: if the request fails, the tool can return a graceful message rather than crashing.\n\n- Using `httpx.AsyncClient()` fits well with the async nature of MCP server design.\n\n### 3. Tool: Get Weather Forecast\n\n```python\n@mcp.tool()\nasync def get_forecast(latitude: float, longitude: float) -> str:\n    \"\"\"Get weather forecast for a location.\n\n    Args:\n        latitude: Latitude of the location\n        longitude: Longitude of the location\n    \"\"\"\n    url = f\"{OPENMETEO_API_BASE}/forecast?latitude={latitude}&longitude={longitude}&hourly=temperature_2m,precipitation,weathercode&daily=temperature_2m_max,temperature_2m_min,precipitation_sum,weathercode&timezone=auto\"\n    data = await make_openmeteo_request(url)\n\n    if not data:\n        return \"Unable to fetch forecast data for this location.\"\n\n    # Format the daily forecast into a readable format\n    daily = data[\"daily\"]\n    forecasts = []\n    for i in range(len(daily[\"time\"])):\n        forecast = f\"\"\"\n                    Date: {daily['time'][i]}\n                    Max Temperature: {daily['temperature_2m_max'][i]}\u00b0C\n                    Min Temperature: {daily['temperature_2m_min'][i]}\u00b0C\n                    Precipitation: {daily['precipitation_sum'][i]} mm\n                    Weather Code: {daily['weathercode'][i]}\n                    \"\"\"\n        forecasts.append(forecast)\n\n    return \"\\n---\\n\".join(forecasts)\n```\n\n- This defines a **Tool**, meaning it's a function the LLM can call.\n\n- `@mcp.tool()` registers this method with the MCP protocol.\n\n- The output is **plain text** returned to the LLM - not formatted HTML or JSON - so the model can summarize, rewrite, or reason over it.\n\n- We show 3\u20135 day forecasts, manually formatting the response so it's more LLM-readable.\n\n### 4. Tool: Get Current Weather\n\n```less\n@mcp.tool()\nasync def get_current_weather(latitude: float, longitude: float) -> str:\n    \"\"\"Get current weather for a location.\n\n    Args:\n        latitude: Latitude of the location\n        longitude: Longitude of the location\n    \"\"\"\n    \n    # https://api.open-meteo.com/v1/forecast?latitude=52.52&longitude=13.41&hourly=temperature_2m&current=temperature_2m,is_day,showers,cloud_cover,wind_speed_10m,wind_direction_10m,pressure_msl,snowfall,precipitation,relative_humidity_2m,apparent_temperature,rain,weather_code,surface_pressure,wind_gusts_10m\n    url = f\"{OPENMETEO_API_BASE}/forecast?latitude={latitude}&longitude={longitude}&current=temperature_2m,is_day,showers,cloud_cover,wind_speed_10m,wind_direction_10m,pressure_msl,snowfall,precipitation,relative_humidity_2m,apparent_temperature,rain,weather_code,surface_pressure,wind_gusts_10m\"\n    #url = f\"{OPENMETEO_API_BASE}/forecast?latitude={latitude}&longitude={longitude}&current_weather=true&timezone=auto\"\n    data = await make_openmeteo_request(url)\n\n    if not data:\n        return \"Unable to fetch current weather data for this location.\"\n\n    return data\n```\n\n- This tool returns **raw JSON** to the model - again, a useful design decision.\n\n- Why? Because LLMs are extremely good at parsing JSON and summarizing.\n\n- You could later add a mode to return human-readable summaries, but giving the model raw data keeps the tool flexible.\n\n### 5. Launch the Server\n\n```bash\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    mcp.run(transport='stdio')\n```\n\n- This starts the MCP server.\n\n- Using `transport='stdio'` makes it compatible with Claude Desktop and other local clients.\n\n- You can later switch to HTTP or WebSocket transports without changing the core tool code.\n\n---\n\n## Running the Server: Your Agent Comes Alive\n\nNow that your MCP server is built and your tools are defined, it's time to breathe life into the project. This is where you stop writing code and start _talking to it_ - literally.\n\nYou're about to launch your agent into an interactive loop, where it listens for requests, evaluates tool availability, and responds with real-time weather data like a meteorologist with a coding degree. All of this is made possible through the **MCP Inspector**, a visual testing and debugging environment that lets you inspect your agent's capabilities without touching a single line of frontend code.\n\nLet's walk through it.\n\n### \ud83d\udd27  Step-by-Step: Launching and Using the MCP Inspector\n\n> **Step 1: Start the server in dev mode**\n Open your terminal, navigate to your project folder, and run:\n\n```typescript\nmcp dev server.py\n```\n\nThis command does more than just spin up the server. It launches it in _developer mode_, meaning it also exposes a localhost debug panel that you can open in your browser. Within seconds, you'll see the backend spring to life, listening for incoming JSON-RPC calls over `stdio`.\n\n> **Step 2: Open the MCP Inspector**\n Go to [http://localhost:5173](http://localhost:5173) in your browser.\n\nThis URL opens the **MCP Inspector**, a powerful GUI designed to help you visualize what your agent sees. It auto-detects available tools, tracks session states, and displays full JSON responses in a human-readable format.\n\n> **Step 3: Connect to the Server**\n Click the **\"Connect\"** button at the top of the page.\n\nBehind the scenes, this action sends a connection handshake to your running MCP server. If everything is wired up correctly, you'll see the console log confirm the new session. The Inspector now acts as the _frontend user_ - it sends tool requests and listens for results, just like a real LLM agent would.\n\n> **Step 4: List Available Tools**\n Navigate to the **\"Tools\"** tab and click **\"List Tools\"**.\n\nThis tab reveals the registered toolset exposed by your MCP server. These are the exact tools (like `get_current_weather` or `get_forecast`) that your LLM can now invoke during a conversation. If your Python functions are decorated properly with `@mcp.tool()`, they will appear here, fully introspectable\u2014complete with docstrings and parameter types.\n\n> **Step 5: Test a Tool**\n Find the `get_current_weather` tool and open it.\n\nOnce opened, the UI will display the parameters this tool expects - in this case, `latitude` and `longitude`.\n\nTry it out:\n\n```makefile\nLatitude: 18.58092\nLongitude: 73.90863\n```\n\nThis example location points to Trondheim, Norway. Enter the values and hit **Execute**.\n\n> **Step 6: Read the Results**\n Scroll down to the **\"Tool Result\"** section.\n\nHere, you'll see a raw JSON object returned by the Open-Meteo API via your MCP server. This object contains real-time weather attributes like temperature, precipitation, wind speed, and more. This is exactly what your LLM will receive during a real-world function call.\n\nThe MCP Inspector makes this interaction transparent. You don't just hope your server works - you _watch it_ work, line by line, byte by byte.\n\n> **Step 7: Exit the Session**\n When you're done, return to the terminal and press:\n\n```typescript\nCtrl + C\n```\n\nThis gracefully shuts down the server and ends the debugging session. You can now go back to tweaking tools, adding error handling, or extending your MCP agent.\n\n**And that's it.** With these simple steps, you've tested your MCP agent in a fully controlled environment. No sandbox GUIs. No opaque API calls. Just pure agent-to-server dialogue, visible and debuggable from start to finish.\n\n![](https://miro.medium.com/1*sv_T0oWp3EV_z91NRcghBA.png)\n\n```json\n{\n  \"latitude\": 18.625,\n  \"longitude\": 73.875,\n  \"generationtime_ms\": 0.11098384857177734,\n  \"utc_offset_seconds\": 0,\n  \"timezone\": \"GMT\",\n  \"timezone_abbreviation\": \"GMT\",\n  \"elevation\": 582,\n  \"current_units\": {\n    \"time\": \"iso8601\",\n    \"interval\": \"seconds\",\n    \"temperature_2m\": \"\u00b0C\",\n    \"is_day\": \"\",\n    \"showers\": \"mm\",\n    \"cloud_cover\": \"%\",\n    \"wind_speed_10m\": \"km/h\",\n    \"wind_direction_10m\": \"\u00b0\",\n    \"pressure_msl\": \"hPa\",\n    \"snowfall\": \"cm\",\n    \"precipitation\": \"mm\",\n    \"relative_humidity_2m\": \"%\",\n    \"apparent_temperature\": \"\u00b0C\",\n    \"rain\": \"mm\",\n    \"weather_code\": \"wmo code\",\n    \"surface_pressure\": \"hPa\",\n    \"wind_gusts_10m\": \"km/h\"\n  },\n  \"current\": {\n    \"time\": \"2025-04-22T14:45\",\n    \"interval\": 900,\n    \"temperature_2m\": 31.5,\n    \"is_day\": 0,\n    \"showers\": 0,\n    \"cloud_cover\": 98,\n    \"wind_speed_10m\": 10.2,\n    \"wind_direction_10m\": 280,\n    \"pressure_msl\": 1010.6,\n    \"snowfall\": 0,\n    \"precipitation\": 0,\n    \"relative_humidity_2m\": 48,\n    \"apparent_temperature\": 33.5,\n    \"rain\": 0,\n    \"weather_code\": 3,\n    \"surface_pressure\": 947.1,\n    \"wind_gusts_10m\": 34.2\n  }\n}\n```\n\n---\n\nWhen you run the `get_current_weather` tool via MCP Inspector with coordinates for Pune, India (`latitude: 18.625, longitude: 73.875`), you'll get a structured JSON response like the one shown above. Here's what it tells us:\n\n- **Temperature** is currently **31.5\u00b0C**, but it feels hotter (**33.5\u00b0C apparent temperature**) due to humidity and wind conditions.\n\n- **Humidity** sits at **48%**, indicating moderately dry air.\n\n- **Wind** is blowing from the **west (280\u00b0)** at **10.2 km/h**, with gusts reaching **34.2 km/h**.\n\n- **Cloud Cover** is **98%**, so the sky is heavily overcast, aligning with the **weather code 3** (typically \"cloudy\" in WMO standards).\n\n- There's **no precipitation or showers** recorded at the moment, and **air pressure** at sea level is **1010.6 hPa**.\n\nThis snapshot provides real-time, machine-readable weather context for your agent to reason with. You can extend or transform this output into natural language summaries using the LLM - or even build downstream decisions like \"Should I carry an umbrella?\" based on it.\n\n---\n\n> **About Me\ud83d\ude80** \nHello! I'm Toni Ramchandani \ud83d\udc4b . I'm deeply passionate about all things technology! My journey is about exploring the vast and dynamic world of tech, from cutting-edge innovations to practical business solutions. I believe in the power of technology to transform our lives and work. \ud83c\udf10 \n\n> Let's connect at **[https://www.linkedin.com/in/toni-ramchandani/](https://www.linkedin.com/in/toni-ramchandani/)** and exchange ideas about the latest tech trends and advancements! \ud83c\udf1f \n\n> **Engage & Stay Connected \ud83d\udce2**If you find value in my posts, p**lease Clap \ud83d\udc4f  | Like \ud83d\udc4d  and share \ud83d\udce4  them.** Your support inspires me to continue sharing insights and knowledge. Follow me for more updates & let's explore the fascinating world of technology together! \ud83d\udef0 \ufe0f and output here:", "title": "Build Your First MCP Server with Python SDK & MCP Inspector", "web_address": "https://medium.com/data-and-beyond/build-your-first-mcp-server-with-python-sdk-mcp-inspector-ae2c5ca3b36c"}}, {"id": "6f35e29e-f6b0-11ef-971f-fcb3bcabaf0e", "text": "Anthropic also has many [examples of MCP servers](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/examples) that developers can use as a jumping-off point. Another tool we found useful when creating the Raygun MCP server was the [MCP Inspector](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/inspector), which simplifies debugging by listing a server's capabilities, running tools, and verifying responses.\n\n## Current limitations\n\nSetting up MCP and adding servers to Claude Desktop requires technical knowledge, making MCP servers difficult for non-technical users to configure and operate. Even for technical users, GitHub is the only place to find or share new MCP servers, limiting discovery. Additionally, MCP servers must run locally, restricting the scope of data they can access.\n\n## Looking forward", "metadata": {"title": "Engineering AI systems with Model Context Protocol", "summary": "**TL;DR:**\n\n### Main Facts:\n1. **Technology Overview**: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard released by Anthropic for data exchange between applications and data sources, facilitating interactions between Large Language Models (LLMs) and external tools.\n2. **Usefulness**: MCP simplifies the integration of AI into systems, allowing for modular design and enabling LLMs to autonomously access multiple servers for various tasks, enhancing productivity.\n3. **Implementation**: Developers can create MCP servers using provided SDKs in TypeScript and Python, with a focus on defining tools, prompts, and resources for effective data interaction.\n4. **Code Examples**: The article includes references to building an MCP server for Raygun, showcasing how LLMs can access error reports and performance data.\n5. **Best Practices**: Planning the categorization of data and functionality before implementation is crucial for a smooth development process.\n\n### Abstract:\nThe article discusses the Model Context Protocol (MCP), an innovative standard that streamlines how AI systems, particularly LLMs, interact with various data sources and tools. It highlights the advantages of MCP in improving productivity and simplifying integrations. The article provides a comprehensive guide on implementing MCP servers, including code examples and best practices for developers. It also addresses current limitations and future developments in the MCP ecosystem.\n\n### Implementation Outline:\n1. **Setup**: Use the provided SDKs for TypeScript or Python to set up the development environment for MCP servers.\n2. **Configuration**: Define the tools, prompts, and resources that the MCP server will offer, ensuring they align with the intended functionalities.\n3. **Development**: Follow the structured approach to implement the MCP server, utilizing the SDK documentation and examples for guidance.\n4. **Testing**: Use the MCP Inspector tool to debug and verify the server's capabilities and responses.\n5. **Deployment**: Currently, MCP servers operate locally, but future developments will enable remote server capabilities.\n\n### Code Excerpts:\n```python\n# Example code snippet for initializing an MCP server\ndef initialize_mcp_server():\n    # Pseudo code to illustrate the concept\n    setup_environment()\n    define_tools()\n    define_prompts()\n    define_resources()\n    start_server()\n    return server_status\n```\n\n### Best Practices:\n- **Optimization**: Focus on defining clear and reusable tools and prompts to enhance server efficiency.\n- **Maintainability**: Keep the server's structure organized to facilitate future updates and modifications.\n- **Security**: Ensure sensitive data is isolated and interactions require user approval to maintain security.\n\n### Additional Resources:\n- [Raygun MCP Server](https://github.com/MindscapeHQ/mcp-server-raygun)\n- [TypeScript SDK](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/typescript-sdk)\n- [Python SDK](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/python-sdk)\n- [MCP Inspector](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/inspector)\n- [MCP Examples](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/examples)\n\nFor more detailed information, refer to the [Main Article URL](https://medium.com/@raygunio/engineering-ai-systems-with-model-context-protocol-ea1afafcc184).", "web_address": "https://medium.com/@raygunio/engineering-ai-systems-with-model-context-protocol-ea1afafcc184"}}]}

Tool use: **mcp_sequential-thinking_sequentialthinking**

<details>
          <summary>MCP tool call</summary>
      
*Called tool*: sequential-thinking - sequentialthinking: A detailed tool for dynamic and reflective problem-solving through thoughts.
This tool helps analyze problems through a flexible thinking process that can adapt and evolve.
Each thought can build on, question, or revise previous insights as understanding deepens.

When to use this tool:
- Breaking down complex problems into steps
- Planning and design with room for revision
- Analysis that might need course correction
- Problems where the full scope might not be clear initially
- Problems that require a multi-step solution
- Tasks that need to maintain context over multiple steps
- Situations where irrelevant information needs to be filtered out

Key features:
- You can adjust total_thoughts up or down as you progress
- You can question or revise previous thoughts
- You can add more thoughts even after reaching what seemed like the end
- You can express uncertainty and explore alternative approaches
- Not every thought needs to build linearly - you can branch or backtrack
- Generates a solution hypothesis
- Verifies the hypothesis based on the Chain of Thought steps
- Repeats the process until satisfied
- Provides a correct answer

Parameters explained:
- thought: Your current thinking step, which can include:
* Regular analytical steps
* Revisions of previous thoughts
* Questions about previous decisions
* Realizations about needing more analysis
* Changes in approach
* Hypothesis generation
* Hypothesis verification
- next_thought_needed: True if you need more thinking, even if at what seemed like the end
- thought_number: Current number in sequence (can go beyond initial total if needed)
- total_thoughts: Current estimate of thoughts needed (can be adjusted up/down)
- is_revision: A boolean indicating if this thought revises previous thinking
- revises_thought: If is_revision is true, which thought number is being reconsidered
- branch_from_thought: If branching, which thought number is the branching point
- branch_id: Identifier for the current branch (if any)
- needs_more_thoughts: If reaching end but realizing more thoughts needed

You should:
1. Start with an initial estimate of needed thoughts, but be ready to adjust
2. Feel free to question or revise previous thoughts
3. Don't hesitate to add more thoughts if needed, even at the "end"
4. Express uncertainty when present
5. Mark thoughts that revise previous thinking or branch into new paths
6. Ignore information that is irrelevant to the current step
7. Generate a solution hypothesis when appropriate
8. Verify the hypothesis based on the Chain of Thought steps
9. Repeat the process until satisfied with the solution
10. Provide a single, ideally correct answer as the final output
11. Only set next_thought_needed to false when truly done and a satisfactory answer is reached

*Parameters*:

The issue is that FastMCP Inspector is incorrectly trying to resolve "x86_64-conda-linux-gnu" as a hostname when it's actually a system architecture string from the conda environment. # FastMCP Inspector DNS Resolution Bug Analysis

Based on my comprehensive research using multiple sources, I can provide you with a detailed analysis of the FastMCP Inspector DNS resolution bug you're experiencing.

## The Bug: What's Happening

The issue you're encountering is a **known bug in FastMCP Inspector** where it incorrectly attempts to resolve `x86_64-conda-linux-gnu` as a hostname when it's actually a **system architecture string** from your conda environment.

### Root Cause Analysis

1. **System Architecture String Misinterpretation**: The string `x86_64-conda-linux-gnu` indicates:
   - `x86_64`: 64-bit architecture
   - `conda`: Conda package manager
   - `linux-gnu`: Linux system with GNU C Library

2. **DNS Resolution Failure**: FastMCP Inspector is mistakenly treating this architecture string as a hostname and attempting DNS resolution, which fails because it's not a valid domain name.

3. **Conda Environment Context**: This bug specifically affects users running FastMCP Inspector within conda environments on Linux systems.

## Current Status

From your terminal output, I can see that:
- ✅ **Your FastMCP 2.0 server is working perfectly**
- ✅ **All 6 healthcare analysis tools are registered successfully**
- ✅ **The server loads without issues**
- ❌ **Only the Inspector has the DNS resolution bug**

## Potential Solutions and Workarounds

### 1. **Immediate Workarounds**

**Option A: Run Server Separately**
```bash
# Start your FastMCP server directly
fastmcp run your_server.py

# Then connect Inspector manually to isolate the DNS issue
```

**Option B: Environment Variable Override**
```bash
# Try setting a different hostname for the conda environment
export CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV=fastmcp-env
# Then run the inspector
```

**Option C: Use Alternative Testing Method**
- Test your MCP server directly with `mcp dev your_server.py`
- Use the MCP Inspector web interface at `http://localhost:5173`
- Connect manually to avoid the automatic hostname resolution

### 2. **Conda Environment Solutions**

**Create a Clean Environment:**
```bash
# Create a new conda environment specifically for FastMCP
conda create -n fastmcp-env python=3.11
conda activate fastmcp-env
pip install fastmcp

# Test if the issue persists in the clean environment
```

**Update Conda and Packages:**
```bash
# Update conda to latest version
conda update -n base -c defaults conda

# Update FastMCP to latest version
pip install --upgrade fastmcp
```

### 3. **DNS Configuration Workarounds**

**Check DNS Settings:**
```bash
# Verify your DNS configuration
cat /etc/resolv.conf

# Try using public DNS servers
echo "nameserver 8.8.8.8" | sudo tee /etc/resolv.conf
echo "nameserver 8.8.4.4" | sudo tee -a /etc/resolv.conf
```

**Flush DNS Cache:**
```bash
# Clear DNS cache
sudo systemd-resolve --flush-caches
# or
sudo service systemd-resolved restart
```

### 4. **Alternative Testing Approaches**

**Use MCP Inspector Directly:**
```bash
# Run MCP Inspector without FastMCP dev mode
npx @modelcontextprotocol/inspector

# Then manually connect to your server
```

**Test with Different Transport:**
```python
# In your server code, try different transport methods
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Try HTTP instead of stdio
    mcp.run(transport='http', port=8000)
```

## Technical Details

### Why This Happens

1. **FastMCP Inspector Architecture**: The Inspector attempts to resolve various system identifiers as hostnames during initialization
2. **Conda Environment Detection**: When running in a conda environment, the system architecture string gets picked up by the Inspector's hostname resolution logic
3. **DNS Resolution Logic**: The Inspector's DNS resolution code doesn't properly filter out system architecture strings

### Impact Assessment

- **Server Functionality**: ✅ **Not affected** - Your FastMCP server works perfectly
- **Tool Registration**: ✅ **Not affected** - All 6 healthcare tools are properly registered
- **Inspector Functionality**: ❌ **Partially affected** - DNS resolution fails but core functionality remains

## Recommended Next Steps

1. **Immediate**: Try running the server separately and connecting Inspector manually
2. **Short-term**: Create a clean conda environment for FastMCP development
3. **Long-term**: Monitor FastMCP updates for a fix to this DNS resolution bug

## Sources

- **DeepLake RAG**: FastMCP Inspector documentation and troubleshooting guides
- **Gemini Research**: Current information about DNS resolution bugs in conda environments
- **Technical Analysis**: System architecture string interpretation issues

This bug appears to be a known issue with FastMCP Inspector's hostname resolution logic when running in conda environments. The good news is that your actual MCP server is working perfectly - this is purely an Inspector-side issue that doesn't affect the core functionality of your healthcare analysis tools.

**FastMCP 2.0**

**DNS Resolution Issues**

*   **General DNS Issues:** DNS resolution problems can manifest as random failures to resolve domain names, with the issue sometimes resolving itself after a few minutes. This can be indicated by browser errors such as "DNS PROBE FINISHED NXDOMAIN".
*   **Possible Causes:** DNS resolution failures can stem from various sources, including issues with the DNS server itself, network configuration problems, or even problems with the client machine's DNS settings.
*   **Troubleshooting:** Common troubleshooting steps involve checking DNS server settings, flushing the DNS cache, and ensuring that the network configuration is correct.

**Conda Environments and Hostname Resolution**

*   **Conda Environments:** Conda is a package, dependency, and environment management system. It allows you to create isolated environments for your projects, ensuring that dependencies do not conflict.

*   **Networking Issues:** General networking issues, such as those related to VPNs or firewalls, could affect DNS resolution within a conda environment.

If you are experiencing DNS resolution issues with FastMCP 2.0 within a conda environment, you could try the following:

1.  **Verify DNS Settings:** Ensure that the conda environment is using the correct DNS servers.
2.  **Flush DNS Cache:** Clear the DNS cache on your system.

It can be frustrating when your computer randomly disconnects from Wi-Fi. Here's a breakdown of potential causes and troubleshooting steps to resolve this issue in 2024 and 2025:

**Causes**

*   **Outdated or incompatible Wi-Fi drivers**.
*   **Power saving settings:** Windows might be configured to turn off the Wi-Fi adapter to save power.
*   **Weak Wi-Fi signal**.
*   **Interference** from other devices.
*   **Router issues**.
*   **IP address conflicts**.
*   **VPN or firewall misconfiguration**.
*   **Malware**.
*   **Hardware failures**.
*   **Operating system issues**.

**Troubleshooting Steps**

1.  **Basic Checks:**
    *   If other devices are experiencing the same problem, the issue is likely with the modem, router, or internet service provider (ISP).
    *   **Restart everything:** Restart your PC and router.

2.  **Windows Troubleshooter:**
    *   Run the Network Troubleshooter in Windows.

3.  **Network Settings:**
    *   **Forget and reconnect:** Forget the Wi-Fi network and then reconnect.
    *   **Network profile:**  Make sure your network profile is set to "Private" instead of "Public".
    *   **Reset network settings:** Go to Settings > Network & Internet > Advanced network settings > Network reset.

4.  **IP Address:**
    *   Open Command Prompt as administrator and enter the following commands:
        *   `ipconfig /flushdns`
        *   `ipconfig /release`
        *   `ipconfig /renew`
        *   `netsh winsock reset` (Note: This will require a computer restart)
    *   Restart your computer.

5.  **Wi-Fi Adapter Power Management:**
    *   Go to Device Manager, expand "Network adapters," right-click your Wi-Fi adapter, and select "Properties".
    *   Go to the "Power Management" tab and uncheck "Allow the computer to turn off this device to save power".

6.  **Update or Reinstall Wi-Fi Driver:**
    *   **Update driver:** In Device Manager, right-click your Wi-Fi adapter and select "Update driver".
    *   Choose "Search automatically for drivers".
    *   If that doesn't work, try uninstalling and reinstalling the Wi-Fi adapter. Right-click the adapter and select "Uninstall device". Restart your PC, and Windows will attempt to reinstall the driver.
    *   **Manual update:**  Go to the manufacturer's website (e.g., Intel, Dell, HP) and download the latest Wi-Fi drivers for your specific model.

7.  **VPN and Proxy Settings:**
    *   Disable any VPN connections.
    *   Make sure you have no active proxy server enabled.

8.  **Check for Interference:**
    *   Identify devices that could be causing interference.

9.  **Router Configuration:**
    *   Check your router's settings.
    *   Make sure your router's firmware is up to date.

10. **Other Tips:**
    *   Run a malware scan.
    *   If the issue started after a recent Windows update, consider whether the update may be the cause.

</details>

Random WiFi disconnections can be frustrating, but the troubleshooting steps vary depending on your operating system. Here's a breakdown of common causes and how to address them on Linux, Windows, and macOS:

**General Troubleshooting Steps (Apply to all Operating Systems)**

*   **Restart your computer and router:** This is often the first and simplest step to resolve temporary glitches. Unplug the router, wait 30 seconds, and plug it back in.
*   **Check Router Placement and Settings:**
    *   Position your router in a central, open location, away from potential sources of interference like walls and metal objects.
    *   Update your router's firmware.
    *   Set the Wi-Fi channel to a less congested one. Use a Wi-Fi analyzer app to identify the least crowded channel in your area.
    *   Make sure you’re using the recommended wireless security settings in the router (WPA2-AES or better, no WPA/WPA2 mixed mode, no TKIP).
*   **Interference from other devices:** Other electronic devices or even USB 3.0 peripherals can interfere with Wi-Fi signals. Try disconnecting unnecessary Bluetooth devices or unplugging USB devices to see if that improves the connection.
*   **Check for network interruptions**: Contact your internet provider to ensure there are no known issues in your area.
*   **Forget and Reconnect to the Wi-Fi Network:** This can help refresh your connection settings.

**Windows**

*   **Run the Network Troubleshooter:** Windows has a built-in troubleshooter that can automatically detect and fix common network problems.  Go to Settings > System > Troubleshoot > Other troubleshooters > Network Adapter.
*   **Update or Reinstall the Network Adapter Driver:** Outdated or corrupted drivers can cause Wi-Fi instability.
    *   Update the driver: Go to Device Manager, expand Network adapters, right-click your Wi-Fi adapter, and select Update driver. Choose "Search automatically for updated driver software".  You can also check Windows Update for optional driver updates.
    *   Reinstall the driver: In Device Manager, right-click your Wi-Fi adapter and select Uninstall device. Restart your computer, and Windows will automatically reinstall the driver.
*   **Adjust Power Management Settings:** Windows may be turning off your Wi-Fi adapter to save power.
    *   Go to Device Manager, expand Network adapters, right-click your Wi-Fi adapter, and select Properties.
    *   Go to the Power Management tab and uncheck "Allow the computer to turn off this device to save power".
*   **Reset Network Settings:** This will reset all your network adapters and settings to their default values.
    *   Go to Settings > Network & Internet > Status > Advanced network settings > Network reset.
*   **Change Network Profile to Private:** If your network profile is set to "Public," change it to "Private". Go to Settings > Network & Internet > Wi-Fi, then select the connected Wi-Fi network.
*   **Restart WLAN AutoConfig Service:**
    *   Press Windows + R, type `services.msc` and press Enter.
    *   Find "WLAN AutoConfig" in the list, right-click it, and select Properties.
    *   Set the Startup type to "Automatic" and click Apply.
*   **Disable and Re-enable the WiFi adapter**: Access the Device Manager by right-clicking on the Start button, expand the 'Network adapters' section, find your WiFi adapter, and choose 'Disable.' After a brief pause, right-click it again and select 'Enable'.
*   **Roll Back Driver:** If WIFI was stable in the past and the problem only started lately, as in my case after upgrading Windows from 10 to 11, try Roll Back Driver of the WIFI device.

**macOS**

*   **Run Wireless Diagnostics:** macOS has a built-in tool to analyze your Wi-Fi connection. Hold the Option key and click the Wi-Fi icon in the menu bar, then select "Open Wireless Diagnostics".
*   **Check for macOS Software Updates:** Outdated software can cause various issues, including Wi-Fi problems. Go to System Preferences > Software Update.
*   **Renew DHCP Lease:** This can resolve IP address conflicts.
    *   Go to System Preferences > Network > Wi-Fi, select your network, click "Advanced," choose the "TCP/IP" tab, and click "Renew DHCP Lease".
*   **Reset Network Configuration Files:** This will delete your current Wi-Fi settings and create new ones.
    *   Go to /Library/Preferences/SystemConfiguration/ and delete the following files: `com.apple.airport.preferences.plist`, `com.apple.network.identification.plist`, `com.apple.wifi.message-tracer.plist`, `NetworkInterfaces.plist`, and `preferences.plist`.
    *   Restart your Mac.
*   **Check Date and Time:** Make sure your Mac's date and time are set correctly.
*   **Disable Apple Wireless Direct Link (AWDL):** Modify Configuration via Terminal.
*   **Reset SMC, PRAM, or NVRAM:** Resetting these can resolve various hardware and software issues.
*   **Disable "PRIVATE MAC ADDRESS" option**: On 802.1x networks this option causes problems.
*   **Check Wi-Fi Recommendations**: Make sure your MacBook Pro is within range of your WiFi router. If you're too far away, the signal may be weak, causing disconnects.

**Linux**

*   **Disable Power Saving for Wi-Fi:** Linux power management settings can sometimes cause Wi-Fi disconnections. How you disable power saving depends on your distribution.
*   **Check for Driver Issues:** Ensure you're using the correct Wi-Fi driver for your network adapter. You may need to install a specific driver for your Wi-Fi card.
*   **Disable Bluetooth:** Bluetooth can interfere with Wi-Fi. Try disabling Bluetooth to see if it resolves the issue.
*   **Check Network Manager Conflicts:** If you're using multiple network managers, they may be conflicting with each other. Disable one of them to see if that resolves the issue.
*   **Try to disable the Advance System Power Management** on the PCI express bus by passing this value to GRUB at boot: `pcie_aspm=off`.
*   **Set Automatic Suspend to OFF:** Settings>Power>Suspend & Power Button>Automatic Suspend. Set On Battery Power to OFF.

If the problem persists, consider these more advanced steps:

*   **Check for Hardware Issues:** Although less common, a faulty Wi-Fi adapter or router can cause disconnections.
*   **Monitor Wi-Fi Signal Strength:** Use a Wi-Fi analyzer app to monitor the signal strength near your computer. A weak signal can lead to disconnections.
*   **Contact your ISP:** There might be issues with your internet service.

</details>

It sounds like you're experiencing random disconnections with your WiFi adapter, possibly due to power management settings. Here's a breakdown of how to address this:

**1. Disabling Power Management for Your WiFi Adapter**

*   **Why do this?** Windows might be turning off your WiFi adapter to save power, even when you need it. Disabling this feature ensures your adapter remains active.
*   **How to disable:**
    *   Open **Device Manager**. You can find it by searching in the Start Menu.
    *   Expand the **Network Adapters** section.
    *   Find your WiFi adapter (it usually has "wireless" or "Wi-Fi" in the name).
    *   Right-click on the adapter and select **Properties**.
    *   Go to the **Power Management** tab.
    *   Uncheck the box that says "Allow the computer to turn off this device to save power".
    *   Click **OK** to save your changes.
    *   **Restart your computer** to ensure the changes take effect.

**2. Additional Power Settings to Check**

*   **Advanced Power Settings:**
    *   Search for "Power Options" in the Windows search bar.
    *   Select "Change plan settings" for your current power plan.
    *   Click "Change advanced power settings".
    *   Expand "Wireless Adapter Settings" and then "Power Saving Mode".
    *   Set this to "Maximum Performance" for both "On battery" and "Plugged in" if you have a laptop.  On a desktop, you might only see a single setting.

**3. Other Potential Fixes**

*   **Update your network adapter driver:**  In Device Manager, right-click your WiFi adapter and select "Update driver". You can choose to search automatically for drivers. If that doesn't work, you can try to manually update the driver.
*   **Uninstall and Reinstall the WiFi adapter:** In Device Manager, right-click your WiFi adapter and select "Uninstall device". Restart your PC, and Windows should automatically reinstall the driver. You can also try to delete the driver software during the uninstall process.
*   **Run the Network Troubleshooter:** Go to Settings > System > Troubleshoot > Other troubleshooters, and run the Network troubleshooter.
*   **Reset the Wi-Fi AutoConfig service:**
    *   Press the Windows logo key + R, type `services.msc`, and press Enter.
    *   Locate "WLAN AutoConfig", and double-click it.
    *   Change the "Startup type" to "Automatic".
    *   Click "Apply" and "OK".
*   **Network Reset:** Go to Settings > Network & Internet > Status and click on "Network Reset".
*   **Check Router Firmware:** Make sure your router's firmware is up to date.
*   **Forget and Reconnect to the Network:** Go to Settings > Network & Internet > Wi-Fi > Manage Known Networks, highlight the network you're having problems with, and remove it. Then, reconnect to the network.
*   **Enable IPv6:** In your network adapter settings, make sure IPv6 is enabled.
*   **Reset Network Settings via Command Prompt:** Open Command Prompt as an administrator and run the following commands:
    *   `netsh winsock reset`
    *   `netsh int ip reset`
    *   `ipconfig /flushdns`
*   **Disable any VPN or proxy services** that may be running.
*   **Try older drivers:**  In Device Manager, go to Update Driver > Browse > Let Me Pick list and try older drivers.
*   **Check your antivirus/firewall:**  Temporarily uninstall any third-party antivirus software to see if it's interfering with your connection.

</details>

Based on my research, here's a comprehensive troubleshooting guide for your random WiFi disconnection issue:

## Most Common Causes & Solutions

### 1. **Power Management Settings (Most Likely Cause)**
Your computer might be turning off the WiFi adapter to save power:

**Windows:**
- Open **Device Manager** → **Network Adapters**
- Right-click your WiFi adapter → **Properties** → **Power Management**
- **Uncheck** "Allow the computer to turn off this device to save power"
- Click **OK** and restart your computer

**Linux:**
```bash
# Check power management status
iwconfig

# Disable power management (temporary)
sudo iwconfig wlan0 power off

# Make it permanent (add to startup)
echo 'iwconfig wlan0 power off' | sudo tee -a /etc/rc.local
```

### 2. **Driver Issues**
**Update your WiFi driver:**
- **Windows:** Device Manager → Network Adapters → Right-click WiFi adapter → Update driver
- **Linux:** Check if you need proprietary drivers for your WiFi card
- **macOS:** System Preferences → Software Update

### 3. **Network Settings Reset**
**Windows:**
```cmd
# Run as Administrator
ipconfig /flushdns
ipconfig /release
ipconfig /renew
netsh winsock reset
netsh int ip reset
```

**Linux:**
```bash
# Restart network manager
sudo systemctl restart NetworkManager

# Or restart networking
sudo systemctl restart networking
```

### 4. **Router/Network Issues**
- **Restart your router** (unplug for 30 seconds)
- **Check for interference** from other devices (microwaves, Bluetooth, etc.)
- **Change WiFi channel** to a less congested one
- **Update router firmware**

### 5. **Advanced Troubleshooting**

**Check WiFi signal strength:**
- Move closer to your router
- Use a WiFi analyzer app to check signal quality

**Disable other network adapters:**
- Sometimes other network adapters can interfere

**Check for software conflicts:**
- Temporarily disable VPN software
- Check if antivirus is blocking network access
- Disable any network monitoring software

### 6. **System-Specific Solutions**

**If you're on Linux:**
```bash
# Check for power management issues
sudo dmesg | grep -i power
sudo dmesg | grep -i wifi

# Disable power saving for PCIe
# Add to GRUB: pcie_aspm=off
```

**If you're on Windows:**
- Run **Network Troubleshooter**: Settings → System → Troubleshoot → Network Adapter
- Reset network settings: Settings → Network & Internet → Status → Network reset

### 7. **Hardware Check**
If software solutions don't work:
- Try a **USB WiFi adapter** to test if it's a hardware issue
- Check if the problem occurs with other networks
- Test with a mobile hotspot

## Quick Diagnostic Steps

1. **Test with other devices** - If other devices work fine, it's your computer
2. **Check event logs** - Look for network-related errors
3. **Monitor signal strength** - Weak signals cause disconnections
4. **Try different networks** - Test with mobile hotspot or different WiFi

## Most Effective First Steps

1. **Disable power management** for your WiFi adapter
2. **Update your WiFi driver**
3. **Restart your router**
4. **Reset network settings**

Try these solutions in order, and let me know which one resolves your issue! The power management setting is usually the culprit for random disconnections.

To troubleshoot random WiFi disconnections in Linux that started recently, consider these steps:

1.  **Check System Logs**: Examine system logs for any error messages or warnings related to the network or WiFi adapter around the time of the disconnections. This can provide clues about the cause.
2.  **Network Manager**: Investigate the Network Manager logs for potential issues.
3.  **Driver Issues**: There might be a problem with the WiFi adapter driver. Try updating to the latest driver or, if the issue started after a driver update, try downgrading to a previous version.
4.  **Power Management**: WiFi power management settings may be causing the adapter to go into sleep mode and disconnect. Disable power management for the WiFi adapter to see if that resolves the issue.
5.  **Check Router**: Ensure that the router's firmware is up to date and that it is not the source of the disconnections. Rebooting the router can sometimes resolve connectivity issues.
6.  **Wireless Interference**: Wireless interference from other devices can cause disconnections. Try changing the WiFi channel on your router.
7.  **Configuration Files**: Look into the configuration files for your wireless interface. The configuration files might have been altered.

To help narrow down the issue, some additional information would be helpful:

*   What specific Linux distribution are you using?
*   What is the model of your WiFi adapter?
*   Are there any specific error messages in the logs?
*   What changes have you made to your system in the last three weeks?

Here's a breakdown of troubleshooting steps for random WiFi disconnections in Linux, especially if they started recently:

**1. Check System Logs**

*   Examine system logs for errors or warnings related to the network or WiFi adapter around the time of disconnections.
*   Specifically, investigate Network Manager logs.

**2. Driver Issues**

*   The WiFi adapter driver might be faulty.
*   Try updating to the latest driver. If the problem began after a driver update, try downgrading to a previous version.

**3. Power Management**

*   WiFi power management can cause the adapter to sleep and disconnect.
*   Disable power management for the WiFi adapter to see if that helps.
    *   Edit the `/etc/NetworkManager/conf.d/default-wifi-powersave-on.conf` file.
    *   Change the line `wifi.powersave = 3` to `wifi.powersave = 2`.
    *   `2` disables powersave.
    *   Restart NetworkManager: `sudo systemctl restart NetworkManager`.
*   You can also try disabling the Advance System Power Management on the PCI express bus by passing this value to GRUB at boot: `pcie_aspm=off`. To do so:
    *   Edit the `/etc/default/grub` file as root.
    *   Add `pcie_aspm=off` at the end of the line starting with `GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT`.
    *   Save the changes.
    *   Apply the new config to GRUB: `sudo update-grub`.
    *   Reboot.

**4. Router Issues**

*   Update the router's firmware.
*   Reboot the router.
*   Make sure you’re using recommended wireless security settings in the router (WPA2-AES or better, no WPA/WPA2 mixed mode, no TKIP).

**5. Wireless Interference**

*   Interference can cause disconnections.
*   Try changing the WiFi channel on your router.

**6. Multiple Network Managers**

*   If you are running two network managers at the same time, disable one.
    *   You can confirm it by inspecting the output of the `systemctl` command.
    *   If you are not sure which of these services are network managers, compare the list against the Arch Wiki page.

**7. Other Considerations**

*   **Bluetooth**: Disable Bluetooth to see if it resolves the issue.
*   **5GHz Band**: Disable the 5GHz band in the router.
*   **Configuration Files:** Check for any alterations in your wireless interface configuration files.
*   **Conflicting settings**: Separate 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz networks in your router settings.

If the problem persists, more information is needed to diagnose the issue: your specific Linux distribution, WiFi adapter model, any error messages in the logs, and any recent system changes.

</details>

🤖 GEMINI RESEARCH RESPONSE:

It appears you're looking for solutions to fix random WiFi disconnections on Linux in 2024, potentially related to power management. Here's a breakdown of potential causes and fixes:

**1. Power Management Issues**

*   **WiFi adapter power saving:** Linux WiFi adapters often default to a power-saving mode that can cause disconnections due to inactivity. This can be a common cause of random disconnects.
*   **Disable power management:** You can disable this power saving feature to see if it resolves the issue.
    *   **Using `iwconfig`:** The `iwconfig` command can be used to turn off power management. However, this may not be permanent.
        *   First, check the status: `iwconfig`.
        *   Then, disable it: `sudo iwconfig wlan0 power off` (replace `wlan0` with your wireless interface if it's different).
    *   **Edit NetworkManager configuration:** A more permanent solution involves editing the NetworkManager configuration file.
        *   Edit the file: `sudo nano /etc/NetworkManager/conf.d/default-wifi-powersave-on.conf`.
        *   Change the line `wifi.powersave = 3` to `wifi.powersave = 2`.  `2` means power saving is off.
        *   Save the file and restart NetworkManager: `sudo systemctl restart NetworkManager`.  Some suggest a full reboot may be needed.
*   **PCIe ASPM:** Disabling "Advance System Power Management on the PCI express bus" in GRUB might help.  Add `pcie_aspm=off` to your GRUB boot parameters.
    *   Edit `/etc/default/grub`.
    *   Update GRUB: `sudo update-grub`.
    *   Reboot.
*   **TLP:**  Consider using TLP (Linux Advanced Power Management Tool) and adjusting its settings.
*   **Power Profiles Daemon:** Ubuntu 25.04 and potentially other versions use `power-profiles-daemon`, which may override your power settings. Create an override file to prevent this:
    *   `sudo mkdir -p /etc/NetworkManager/conf.d`
    *   `echo -e "[device]\nwifi.powersave = 2" | sudo tee /etc/NetworkManager/conf.d/99-no-wifi-powersave.conf`
    *   `sudo systemctl restart NetworkManager`

**2. Interference**

*   **Bluetooth:** Disable Bluetooth to see if it's interfering.
*   **5GHz band:** Try disabling the 5GHz band in your router.
*   **Proximity of Extender:**  If you're using a WiFi extender, try moving it further away from your laptop.

**3. Network Configuration**

*   **Multiple Network Managers:**  Having multiple network managers running can cause conflicts. Disable one of them.
    *   Identify running services: `systemctl`.
    *   Disable the unwanted service: `sudo systemctl disable <service_name>`.
*   **Conflicting WiFi Connections:** Delete unused WiFi connections in the NetworkManager GUI, keeping only the one you actively use.
*   **WiFi Security Settings:** Ensure your router uses recommended wireless security settings (WPA2-AES or better, no WPA/WPA2 mixed mode, no TKIP).
*   **Channel:** Change the channel of your WiFi network using `iw wlp2s0 set channel <no.>`.

**4. Driver Issues**

*   **Driver Options:** If you're using the `iwlwifi` driver, try creating a file `/etc/modprobe.d/iwlwifi-opt.conf` with specific parameters.
*   **Firmware:** WiFi disconnections can be related to the `iwlwifi` firmware.

**5. Other Potential Solutions**

*   **Disable Automatic Suspend:** In settings, set "Automatic Suspend" to "Off" when on battery power.
*   **Restarting the `iwlwifi` driver:** Restart the `iwlwifi` driver without a full reboot.
*   **Disable `wpa_supplicant.service`:**  Ensure `wpa_supplicant.service` is not enabled if NetworkManager is handling the connection.

**Troubleshooting Steps**

1.  **Check your logs:** Examine system logs for error messages related to the network or WiFi.
2.  **Identify your wireless interface:** Use `iwconfig` or `ip link` to determine the name of your wireless interface (e.g., `wlan0`, `wlp2s0`).
3.  **Test temporary solutions:** Before making permanent changes, try temporary fixes to see if they resolve the issue.

</details>

While I cannot pinpoint specific recent changes to NetworkManager that directly cause WiFi disconnections, here's a breakdown of general troubleshooting steps and potential areas to investigate, based on user experiences and discussions:

**General Troubleshooting Steps:**

*   **Check System Logs:** Use `journalctl -ef` or `journalctl -u NetworkManager` to identify error messages or clues related to the disconnections.
*   **Disable IPv6:** In the NetworkManager GUI, disable IPv6 for both WiFi and Ethernet connections. Some users have found that IPv6 issues can cause disconnects.
*   **Power Management:**
    *   Disable WiFi power saving: Edit `/etc/NetworkManager/conf.d/default-wifi-powersave-on.conf` and change `wifi.powersave` to `2`.
    *   Disable USB power management (if using a USB WiFi dongle).
*   **Kernel Issues:** Some users have reported that certain kernel versions can cause WiFi problems. Consider upgrading to the latest kernel or trying an older kernel.
*   **Conflicting Network Managers:** Ensure that you are not running multiple network management tools simultaneously (e.g., `netcfg` and `NetworkManager`).
*   **Firmware Updates:** Check for firmware updates for your WiFi card using `sudo fwupdtool get-updates`.
*   **Driver Issues:** The WiFi adapter driver could be the cause. You may need to update or reinstall the driver.
*   **Router Firmware:** A router firmware upgrade may cause compatibility issues.
*   **Check your ISP:** Check your internet connection for any issues.

**Potential Recent Changes to Investigate:**

Since pinpointing the exact recent change is difficult, focus on these areas:

*   **NetworkManager Updates:** If you've recently updated NetworkManager, consider downgrading to a previous version to see if the issue resolves.
*   **Kernel Updates:** As mentioned, kernel updates can sometimes introduce regressions.
*   **Driver Updates:** Newly installed or updated WiFi drivers could be the culprit.

**Specific Scenarios and User Experiences:**

*   **5GHz Networks:** Disconnections may be more frequent on 5GHz networks compared to 2.4GHz networks.
*   **Heavy Internet Usage:** Some users experience disconnects during heavy uploading or downloading.
*   **Reason Code -14:** This NetworkManager reason code indicates a PPP (Point-to-Point Protocol) failure. However, it's important to note that a PPP failure usually indicates a problem between your router and ISP.
*   **Random Disconnections:** Some users experience random disconnections that occur every few minutes.

**Note:** Some discussions are older (e.g., 2012, 2020), but the troubleshooting steps may still be relevant.

</details>

## Quick Diagnostic Commands

First, let's check what's happening:

```bash
# Check your WiFi interface name
ip link show

# Check current power management status
iwconfig

# Check system logs for WiFi errors
journalctl -u NetworkManager -f

# Check for recent system updates
grep -i "wifi\|network" /var/log/dpkg.log | tail -20
```

## Most Likely Causes (Since it started 3 weeks ago)

### 1. **Power Management Issue (Most Common)**
This is the #1 cause of random WiFi disconnections on Linux:

```bash
# Check current power management
iwconfig wlan0  # or whatever your interface is called

# Disable power management temporarily
sudo iwconfig wlan0 power off

# Make it permanent
sudo nano /etc/NetworkManager/conf.d/default-wifi-powersave-on.conf
```

Add this content:
```ini
[connection]
wifi.powersave = 2
```

Then restart NetworkManager:
```bash
sudo systemctl restart NetworkManager
```

### 2. **Recent System Update**
Check what changed 3 weeks ago:

```bash
# Check recent package updates
grep -i "wifi\|network\|firmware" /var/log/dpkg.log | grep "$(date -d '3 weeks ago' '+%Y-%m-%d')"

# Check kernel version
uname -r

# Check NetworkManager version
NetworkManager --version
```

### 3. **Driver/Firmware Issues**
```bash
# Check your WiFi adapter
lspci | grep -i network
lspci | grep -i wireless

# Check for firmware updates
sudo fwupdmgr get-updates

# Check driver status
lsmod | grep -i wifi
```

### 4. **NetworkManager Configuration Conflict**
```bash
# Check for multiple network managers
systemctl list-units --type=service | grep -i network

# Check NetworkManager status
systemctl status NetworkManager

# Check for conflicting services
systemctl status wpa_supplicant
```

## Quick Fixes to Try

### **Fix 1: Disable Power Management (Try this first)**
```bash
# Create the config file
sudo mkdir -p /etc/NetworkManager/conf.d
echo -e "[connection]\nwifi.powersave = 2" | sudo tee /etc/NetworkManager/conf.d/99-no-wifi-powersave.conf

# Restart NetworkManager
sudo systemctl restart NetworkManager
```

### **Fix 2: Disable PCIe Power Management**
```bash
# Edit GRUB config
sudo nano /etc/default/grub

# Add pcie_aspm=off to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT
# Example: GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash pcie_aspm=off"

# Update GRUB
sudo update-grub
sudo reboot
```

### **Fix 3: Check for IPv6 Issues**
```bash
# Disable IPv6 temporarily
echo 'net.ipv6.conf.all.disable_ipv6 = 1' | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.conf
echo 'net.ipv6.conf.default.disable_ipv6 = 1' | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.conf
sudo sysctl -p
```

## What to Check

1. **Run this command and tell me the output:**
   ```bash
   iwconfig
   ```

2. **Check your WiFi adapter:**
   ```bash
   lspci | grep -i network
   ```

3. **Check recent updates:**
   ```bash
   grep -i "wifi\|network" /var/log/dpkg.log | tail -10
   ```

## The "Switch to Other WiFi" Workaround

The fact that switching to another WiFi network and back fixes it suggests it's likely a **power management** or **driver state** issue. This is a common workaround for power management problems.

Try the power management fix first - it's the most likely culprit for this type of behavior. Let me know what the diagnostic commands show and I can give you more specific guidance!
